coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of mustard seeds carbon dots and study on fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qingmei
He, Songjie
Zheng, Kaiwen
Zhang, Lupeng
Lin, Lihua
Chen, Fenghua
Du, Xiujuan
Li, Bing
coredata.dc:description: A growinginteresthasemergedin carbon dot (CDs) fluorescent probes,astheyarenon-destructive, non-contact, sensitive, fast, and low-cost. However, the low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and the uncertainty of fluorescence quenching mechanism are the main bottlenecks restricting its application. Here, we used mustard seeds as carbon source, CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method withoutfurthersurface treatment with the QY reached a value as high as 17.92 % in 468 nm excitation energy. Asalabel-freefluorescentprobe,CDscanbeusedto sense Fe3+ ions in environmental water andhuman oral keratinocytes (Hok) cells, with a detection limit of 0.51 μM and the linear range of 0–75 μM. Moreover, we have conducted an in-depth study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by Fe3+ ions. Our study provides powerful evidences that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching to Fe3+ ionsis a synergistic effect of static quenching (SQ) and aggregation-induced effect (AIE), which accompanied by forminga non-fluorescent ground state complex between CDs and Fe3+ ions. This work promises to provide new ideas for the most appropriate detection strategy and design the best performing sensor, allowing their effective application in the sensing field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mustard seeds
Fe3+ ions
Fluorescent probes
Quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in environmental water
Cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) cell viability assay
Fluorescence properties and stability of the samples
In vitro cell imaging of the samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs (Inset: corresponding histogram size distribution measured from TEM images), (b) HRTEM of CDs, (c) XRD pattern of CDs.
(a) XPS survey, (b) high-resolution spectra of CDs for C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, (e) S2p, and (f) FTIR spectra.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra, excitation and emission spectra of CDs solutions, (b) The fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 425 to 525 nm, (c) The fluorescence intensity ratio of F/F0 of CDs versus pH values, (d) The fluorescence intensity ratio of F/F0 of CDs versus NaCl concentration under 468 nm excitation.

(a) Fluorescence response of CDs to different metal ions, (b) Fluorescence response of CDs solution upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions, (c) The fluorescence intensity ratio of F0/F of CDs versus concentrations of Fe3+ ions, Inset shows the linear response in the range of 10 μm-75 μm, (d) Fluorescence response of CDs solution upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ in river water.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe, CDs, and CDs/Fe, (b) the fluorescence decay curve of the CDs and Fe/CDs upon excitation at 468 nm, (c) FTIR spectra of CDs and Fe/CDs, (d) XPS survey spectra of CDs and Fe/CDs, (e) the XRD Pattern of CDs with and without of Fe3+ ions, (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ ions solution (blank line), excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of CDs solutions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) TEM image of CDs, and (b) TEM image of Fe/CDs. (c) SEM image of CDs, and (d) SEM image of Fe/CDs.

(a) Bright-field images of Hok cells in the absence of CDs (b) presence of CDs, (c) Fluorescent image of CDs under excitation at 488 nm, (d) Bright-field images of Hok cells in the presence of Fe3+ ions, (e) and (f) Fluorescent image of CDs incubated with Fe3+ ions (20 μm) and (40 μm) under excitation at 488 nm.
The schematic diagram for the preparation of CDs and the mechanism of fluorescence quenching to Fe3+ ions.
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of CDs to the determination of Fe3+ in River Water.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of mustard seeds carbon dots and study on fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Applied Science, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Taiyuan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Applied Science, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of physics, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hospital of Stomatology, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hospital of Stomatology, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1. The schematic diagram for the preparation of CDs and the mechanism of fluorescence quenching to Fe3+ ions.

A growinginteresthasemergedin carbon dot (CDs) fluorescent probes,astheyarenon-destructive, non-contact, sensitive, fast, and low-cost. However, the low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and the uncertainty of fluorescence quenching mechanism are the main bottlenecks restricting its application. Here, we used mustard seeds as carbon source, CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method withoutfurthersurface treatment with the QY reached a value as high as 17.92 % in 468 nm excitation energy. Asalabel-freefluorescentprobe,CDscanbeusedto sense Fe3+ ions in environmental water andhuman oral keratinocytes (Hok) cells, with a detection limit of 0.51 μM and the linear range of 0–75 μM. Moreover, we have conducted an in-depth study on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by Fe3+ ions. Our study provides powerful evidences that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching to Fe3+ ionsis a synergistic effect of static quenching (SQ) and aggregation-induced effect (AIE), which accompanied by forminga non-fluorescent ground state complex between CDs and Fe3+ ions. This work promises to provide new ideas for the most appropriate detection strategy and design the best performing sensor, allowing their effective application in the sensing field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) fluorescent probes have emerged as a hot topic due to their attractive advantages, such as non-destructive, non-contact, sensitive, fast, low-cost, and simple fabrication. Among many kinds of analytes, the detection of metal ions was the most widely studied [1–8]. As a good fluorescent probe, it should have high sensitivity, specific selectivity, fast response speed, wide linear response range, and good stability. To achieve this, it is crucial to figure out the fluorescence quenching mechanism. In this regard, Wu et al. used traditional Chinese herbal medicine as carbon source to hydrothermally synthesize CDs with blue fluorescence for the first time. By adding Hg2+ ion strong coupling agent ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA) to CDs/Hg2+ mixed solution, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was reduced by less than 5 %. It is proved that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by Hg2+ ion is Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), which is due to coupling of COO– functional group on CDs surface with Hg2+ ion [9]. For the CDs derived from bergamot [10]and mangosteen [11], the static quenching effect (SQE) and aggregation-induced effect (AIE) were proofed by means of fluorescence lifetime and transmission electron microscope (TEM), respectively. Recently, the quenching mechanism based synergistic effect have been increasingly surfaced. For instance, Liao et al. fabricated N-CDs based on the one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of the mixture of peach gum polysaccharide (PGP) and ethylenediamine (EDA) in mild conditions. The prepared N-CDs quenched the Au3+ ions via the synergic effect of ET and FRET, which is confirmed by ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum (UV–vis) and TEM [12]. Lu et al. prepared S, N/CDs using bean pods and onion as precursors through a simple, economical, and environmentally friendly strategy. The obtained S, N/CDs could be selectively quenched by Ag+ ion through the synergistic mechanism of SQE and AIE, although the author provided some proofs by means of UV–vis, TEM, Zeta potential, and fluorescence lifetime measurements [13], the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is still not well understood. It can be seen that great efforts have been made in CDs-based fluorescent probes [9–18], from the addition of strong coupling agents firstly to the use of various characterization methods later. However, the exact mechanism of fluorescence quenching is still unclear. Therefore, there remains wide space in exploring the mechanism of CDs based fluorescence probe.

Address the above issues and be inspired by the literature [19], we present a simple, low cost and green approach to obtain highly fluorescent CDs from mustard seeds by hydrothermal treatment.The obtained CDs exhibits good fluorescence properties with a high quantumyield (QY) of 17.92 %. Meanwhile, it demonstrated stable fluorescence when exposed to a strongly acidic, alkaline environment, and a wide temperature range, which is beneficial for practical applications. It can serve as an effective fluorescent probe for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in ambient water and Human oral keratinocytes (Hok) successfully, with a detection limit of 0.51 μM and the linear range of 0–75 μM. What is more significant is that we have conducted an in-depth study on the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs caused by Fe3+ ions with UV–vis, FTIR, XPS, XRD, SEM, TEM, and fluorescent lifetime measurements. It was proved that the mechanism of fluorescence quenchingis synergisticeffect of aggregation-induced and static quenching, which caused by forminga non-fluorescent ground state complex between CDs and Fe3+ ions. The synthesis and fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs are briefly summarized in Scheme 1 . This work may be provided some reference for understanding the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs-based fluorescent probes.

The mustard seeds were purchased from a vegetable market in Taiyuan, China. Other chemicals reagent used in this work included Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, SrCO3, MnSO4·H2O, Co(NO3)2·4H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, BaCl2, AgNO3, CaCl2·2H2O, KCl, ZnCl2, La(NO3)3·9H2O, NaCl, LiNO3, and HgCl2 were bought from Aladdin Chemistry Co. ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals reagent were of analytical grade and were used just as they were received, with no further purification. By dissolving the appropriate metal salts in ultrapure water, stock solutions of Fe3+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Ag+, Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, La3+, Na+, Li+, and Hg2+ were created. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin mixture, phosphate buffer saline, Roswell park memorial institute 1640, Trypsin, and cell counting kit-8 were purchased from Amresco, USA. Hok cells were provided by Shanghai Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Science.

In a typical synthesis route, mustard seeds were thoroughly washed with ultrapure water before being dried and grounded into powder. Themustardseedspowderismixedwithwateraccordingtoaspecificratio and thentransferred to aTeflon linedautoclave.The autoclave was heated to 180 °C for 10 h before being allowed to cool naturally. To remove the contaminants and big particles, the dark-brown solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant containing CDs was then dialyzed against ultrapure water for 72 h using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1 kDa), and the purified CDs solution was lyophilized. Finally, the obtained product was gathered for future use.

The Philips Tecnai G2 microscope (Philips, Netherlands) was used to obtain high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) pictures at 200 kV. With a Bruker D8 powder diffractometer, XRD patterns were obtained for CDs with a Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.5406 A) at a scan rate of 5°/min from 10° to 80°. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB XI with a monochromatic Al-K X-ray source at a pressure of 5 × 10-10 mbar. UV–vis absorption was measured in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm using a TU-1910 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer. Thermo scientific Nicolet iS50 was used to record Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra. To measure the fluorescence spectrum, a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog 3–22 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used. We also determined the absolute QY using an integrating sphere and a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments).Fluorescence lifetime decay profile was studied on the same instrument as fluorescence QY using a Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) Spectrometer. An inverted fluorescent microscope was used for the cell imaging experiments (IX51, OLYMPUS).

The as-prepared CDs encouraged us to study their potential sensing applications due to their superior optical properties. All of the metal ion stock solutions were made from their salts. In each experiment, 0.2 mL of metal ion solution with a concentration of 0.5 mM was mixed with CDs (1.8 mL).After incubation for 20 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 468 nm. For the sensitivity test, 0.2 mL Fe3+ solutions of varying concentrations were mixed with 1.8 mL CDs solution. The fluorescence spectra were measured under the identical conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in environmental water
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of detecting Fe3+ ions in environmental water, samples of the Fenhe river (Taiyuan, China) were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane before measurement.The CDs solution (1.8 mL) was then spiked with standard Fe3+ ions at concentrations of 20 μM and 40 μM (0.2 mL). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution was then measured in order to determine the amounts of Fe3+ ions in ambient water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) cell viability assay
ce:section.ce:para: The CCK-8 cytotoxicity experiment was carried out on Hok cells with varying CDs concentrations. To begin, the Hok cells were planted at a density of 104 cells per well on a 96-well plate with a medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum, 1 % penicillin–streptomycin mixture, and 1640 media in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C. Replace the original medium with fresh media containing varying concentrations of CDs solution ranging from 0 to 800 μg /L (0, 25, 50, 100, 400, and 800 μg /L), and cultivate for another 24 h. After removing the medium as mentioned above, each well received 100 μL of CCK-8 solution (AR1160, Boster) and was incubated for 1 h. A microplate reader was used to measure the absorbance at 410 nm (SPECTRA MAX PLUS 384).

Hok cells were planted in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 per well to investigate the cell imaging capabilities of CDs. After 24 h of incubation, the cells were treated with 400 μg/mL CDs for 24 h. The cells were then washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove the medium and free CDs. In order to detect Fe3+, 20 μM (or 40 μM) of Fe3+ ion solution was added to cells treated with CDs and incubated for 2 h. Finally, an inverted fluorescent microscope was used to image the cells.

The Morphology, size and microstructure of CDs prepared under optimal synthetic conditions were assessed by TEM and XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the TEM image of as-prepared CDs shows spherical particles with good dispersion. The corresponding particle size distribution histograms indicate that these CDs have diameters ranging from 0.5 to 3.5 nm, with an average diameter of 2 nm (inset).Such small and homogeneous particle sizes could be useful for in-vivo cell imaging applications. The HRTEM picture of CDs shows noticeable lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.22 nm in Fig. 2 b, which is consistent with the literature [20]. The XRD diffraction pattern illustrated a broad diffraction peak at about 21° as shown in Fig. 2c, which is a distinctive peak of CDs and corresponded to graphite's (002) crystal plane[21]. These results indicated that the obtained CDs were amorphous and graphitic in nature.

To get a clear information regarding the surface states and chemical composition of CDs, XPS and FTIR analysis were performed. As shown in Fig. 2a, the full range of XPS spectrum showed that peak located at 163.6, 284.8, 399.8, and 532 eV were attributed to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The XPS analysis of the elemental composition of CDs demonstrated that they were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur, with atomic percentages of 80.13, 13.65, 5.48, and 0.74 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectra shown in Fig. 2b, demonstrates that the binding energy peak at 284.3 eV was attributed to CC, 285.0 eV and 286.1 eV correspond to the CC, and CO/CN [22,23], respectively, while the peak around 288.2 eV could be assigned to CO [24]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the spectrum of the O1s element indicated that the peak of 530.9 eV ascribed to C-OH, while the peak of 531.9 eV and 532.8 eV indicated the presence of COC, and CO groups, respectively [25,26]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum was shown in Fig. 2d, the peak located at 398.3 eV, 400.1 eV, and 401.3 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N [27], respectively. This further demonstrated the existence of nitrogen-containing functional groups in CDs. The S2p spectra are dominated by two peaks with centered binding energy of 163.7 eV and 168.8 eV, indicating that sulfur atoms exist in two states: thiophene-S and sulfate/sulfonate [28], as shown in Fig. 2e. The FTIR spectra were used to investigate the functional groups of CDs further. The absorption bands at 3249-1 and 1561-1 in Fig. 2f are attributed to stretching vibrations of –OH/NH groups [20,29]. The other peaks in 2940-1, 1394-1, 2354-1, and 1078-1 correspond to stretching vibrations of CH [20], COC [29,30], CN, and CO groups [31], respectively. The XPS and FTIR results confirm each other, revealing that the surface of the CDs is rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl, and nitrogen-containing functional groups. In the meantime, it suggested that the CDs are water-soluble and easy to functionalize.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and stability of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CDs were investigated by measuring UV − vis and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the two characteristic absorption peaks are located at 276 nm and 321 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The peak at 276 nm could be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds, another peak at about 321 nm was possibly caused by the n-π* transitions of CO group on the surface of CDs. A digital picture of CDs taken in sunlight and UV light were shown in the top inset of Fig. 3a. It is quite easy to see CDs dispersing in water and emitting green fluorescence. To assess the optical properties of the as-synthesized CDs, the emission and excitation spectrum were also examined, as shown in Fig. 3a. Fluorescence excitation spectrums appear as solid red lines, with optimal excitation centered around 468 nm. The emission spectra were also presented in Fig. 3a with a solid blue line, which showed a strong peak at 543 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission as seen in Fig. 3b, which is similar to the literature [32]. When the CDs were excited at wavelengths from 425 nm to 465 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity increased and the corresponding emission peaks shifted from 520 to 540 nm. As the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases and emission peak shifts to a longer wavelength. The optimal excitation wavelength was at 465 nm associated with the strongest emission peak at around 540 nm. The nature of this excitation dependence was due to CDs having different size and surface states [33]. The absolute QY of CDs was determined to be 17.94 % under an excitation wavelength of 468 nm with an integrating sphere. The stability is essential for both environmental and clinical applications. Initially, the effect of pH variation on the fluorescence properties of CDs was studied. As seen in Fig. 3c, the fluorescence intensity of CDs changed little across a wide pH range of 4 to 10. Meanwhile, the influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence behavior of CDs was examined, as shown in Fig. 3d. The level of fluorescence intensity of CDs remains constant even when the NaCl concentration is increased to 1.0 M. The study indicates that CDs can withstand a variety of pH values and ionic strengths. The effect of ambient temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was also examined. The fluorescence intensity of CDs varies by less than 20 % over a wide temperature range of 0-100℃, as shown in Fig S1. That is, the average fluorescence intensity varies by less than two thousandths per degree. Such small changes can be ignored. We also noticed that the CDs exhibited marvelous reversibility and restorability whenever the temperature changed. In other words, the temperature (0–100℃) cannot cause permanent destruction on the surface fluorescent structure of CDs. It is further evidence of the stability of the CDs we obtained.

The excellent stability and abundant surface functional groups of CDs inspired us to study their applications in metal ion sensing. The CDs solution (1.8 mL) was mixed with a solution containing various metal ions at a concentration of 0.5 mM (0.2 mL), and the fluorescence spectra was obtained at 468 nm excitation wavelength. According to Fig. 4 a, when Fe3+ ion was added to CDs, the fluorescence intensity dropped by 90 %, whereas other metal ions only caused a small decline. This finding suggests that CDs have a high selectivity for Fe3+ ions. The selective fluorescence response can be ascribed to the high binding affinity of the surface functional groups of CDs towards Fe3+ ions over others. As we know, the property of CDs is dependent on their chemical structure. So, the main species of functional group on the surface of CDs affect their specific recognition of metal ions. The emission spectra of CDs in the presence of different amounts of Fe3+ ions were recorded to illustrate the sensitivity of CDs as a fluorescence probe. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of CDs reduced with the amounts of Fe3+ ions increasing in Fig. 4b. For the quantitative analysis of the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of Fe3+ ions, we plotted the quenching efficiency (F/F0) versus the concentration of Fe3+ ions from 0 μM to 1000 μM, as shown in Fig. 4c. Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively. In the lower concentration range (0–75 μM), the inset of Fig. 4c displays a good linear character, with linear regression equations of F/F0 = 0.0044c + 0.9823 (R2 = 0.9915), while deviates from the linear relationship at higher concentrations (100 μM-1000 μM). Based on the equation 3σ/m, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.51 μM, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 11) and m is the slope of the linear fit. This result is superior or comparable to other fluorescent probes described in the literature [20,34–36,38–41], as listed in Table 1 .

Impressed by their fascinating analytical features like high sensitivity and selectivity, the analytical performance of the CDs based Fe3+ detection system is further evaluated in real samples. For this analysis, we collected Fenhe river for environmental water sample testing. Prior to measurement, the water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles and insoluble matter. Then the samples were spiked with different amounts of Fe3+ ions, analysed by the similar procedure and the recoveries obtained from the standard curve (Fig. 4c) were provided in Fig. 4d and Table 2 . For each Fe3+ ion concentration, we measured five times in parallel in order to make sure the reliability of the data. Similar to the result of Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased almost in proportional relationship after adding the Fe3+ ions. As shown in the Table 2, great recoveries (101.8 to 105 %) with decent RSD values (less than1%) were obtained, demonstrating the potential of the present sensing system for the detection of Fe3+ ions in environmental as well as biological samples.

Understanding the photoluminescence quenching mechanism of the CDs is fundamental in order to exploit at the maximum of their possibilities, allowing to identify the most appropriate detection strategy and design the best performing sensor. Fluorescence quenching can take place in a variety of ways, including DQ, SQ or a combination of the two (DQ and SQ). The quenching mechanism can vary from case to case depending on the composition, surface functionalization of CDs, and the nature of the quencher. Seven different mechanisms are known for CDs quenching [15]. As we know, the excited states of CDs are affected by the dynamic quenching process, not the ground state. Therefore, the absorbance spectra are not likely to alter during dynamic quenching. When a complex forms in the ground state during static quenching, changes in the CDs absorbance spectra are to be expected. As displayed in the absorption spectrum of Fig. 5 a, both the position and intensity of the absorption peak have changed dramatically, indicating the formation of a non-radiative recombination ground state matter [11]. As a piece of direct evidence to distinguish between DQ and SQ, the fluorescence lifetime of the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions were also measured with time-resolved spectroscopic study. Fig. 5b shows that the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs was unchanged by the addition of Fe3+ ions, indicating a SQ mechanism. Even if the fluorescence lifetime is the main discerning parameter between DQ and SQ, a series of other experiments can be performed to further investigate the quenching mechanism. Thereupon, FTIR, XPS, and XRD of the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions were done. The results were illustrated in Fig. 5c, 5d, and 5e. By comparing the FTIR spectrum, it is not difficult to see that the peak intensity of the CO bond is significantly enhanced when the Fe3+ ions were added. This conclusion is consistent with the literature [20]. Meanwhile, it is more noticeable in XPS survey spectra, as seen in Fig. 5d. When Fe3+ ions were introduced, the amount of C1s decreased by 28.6 % while the content of O1s increased by 1.17 times. We also found a binding energy peak linked with Fe3+ ions in XPS survey spectra. In addition, as demonstrated in Fig. S2, the peak areas of CO functional groups are significantly increased when compared to CDs without Fe3+ ions, no matter whether they are high-resolution C1s or O1s. This result is consistent with FTIR, and is a further proof of the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between CDs and Fe3+ ions. Fig. 5e shows that the diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns have been substantially broadened and have migrated to high angles, indicating that the electronic structure has changed after Fe3+ ions introduced. To put it another way, it implied that Fe3+ ions change the electronic structure of the CDs by forming a stable non-fluorescence ground state complexes or chelates between surface functional groups and Fe3+ ions [42]. These results strongly support the SQ, which resulted by ground-state complex formation between the surface functional groups of CDs and Fe3+ ions.

There is still a possibility that the quenching could derive from an aggregation phenomenon among nanoparticles, induced by the complex formation. In order to confirm this scenario, TEM and SEM were used to characterize the obtained CDs with and without Fe3+ ions. Once Fe3+ ions were added, the aggregation morphology was observed, as shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b. The results clearly showed that CDs aggregated in the presence of Fe3+ ions, which manifested as an aggregation morphology of multiple CDs with greater diameters. SEM, as an intuitive means to characterize the morphology of samples, was also used. Compared to Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d, a significant agglomeration phenomenon had observed as soon as Fe3+ ions were introduced, which produced by the CDs and Fe3+ ions combining. To clarify the nature of this phenomenon, we selected multiple areas for observation, and the results were without exception (Fig. S3). As reported by literature [42], Fe3+ has been shown to coordinate with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the edge of CDs, resulting in severe aggregation of CDs with each other. As for FRET and IFE, since there is no overlap between the absorption spectra of the quencher and the emission/excitation spectra of the emitting CDs (Fig. 4f), it can be excluded. Taken together, it is evident that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by Fe3+ ion is mostly attributed to synergisticeffect of AIE and SQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell imaging of the samples
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibilityof CDs must be considered as a potential bio-labeling reagent. The viability of cell was evaluated by varying the concentrations of CDs (Fig. S4). Based on the data presented, it appears that the CDs exhibit very low cytotoxicity, with a viability of more than 85 percent even at an 800 μg/mL concentration, while viability of around 100 percent at a 200 μg/mL concentration after 24 h incubation. This result is to be expected because our carbon source and synthesis method are both green and no chemical reagents are used in the synthesis process. As a result, the CDs could be used securely for in vitro cell imaging. Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7b show bright-field pictures in the absence and presence of CDs (200 μg/mL). The CDs always fluoresced red and significantly tagged both the cell membrane and the cytoplasm of Hok cells, as seen in Fig. 7c. Typically, the fluorescence color of CDs fluctuates with concentration. To illustrate the rationality of this result, Fig. S5a depicts the excitation and emission spectra of CDs (1:3, the ratio between mustard seeds and ultrapure water before reaction), whereas Fig. S5b is the corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates derived from the emission spectra of CDs. At higher reaction concentrations, CDs show nearly red fluorescence. Given the high concentration of CDs (0.4 mg/mL) in our cell imaging experiments, it seems probable that CDs have persistent red fluorescence independent of excitation wavelength. On the one hand, it can reduce the blue autofluorescence of the cell tissue itself [43], and on the other hand, the excitation light source is not limited to make the application more convenient. These findings demonstrate that CDs have great potential for biological applications, implying that they might be used for direct and efficient cell labeling.

Fe3+ ions are indispensable for many living systems and play an essential role in metabolic activities. Detecting Fe3+ ions via a visible fluorescent technique would be extremely advantageous due to its non-invasive nature. Considering the high QY of CDs and the selective quenching of Fe3+ ions, we further employed them for intracellular detection of Fe3+ ions. Fig. 7d is the bright-field image of CDs with Fe3+ ions added. The photos of CDs with concentrations of 20 μM and 40 μM Fe3+ ions were shown in Fig. 7e and Fig. 7f. There was modest red fluorescence when 20 μM Fe3+ ions were provided. However, fluorescence was barely observable when 40 μM Fe3+ ions were added. It implies that Fe3+ ions can quench the fluorescence of CDs more efficiently in biological systems than in ambient water, which could be owing to the pH difference between the cell and the ambient water [44]. A preliminary analysis was conducted in the feasibility of using CDs to sense Fe3+ ions in cells, and it was determined that CDs may be used as fluorescent markers to sense the concentration of Fe3+ ions in cells to a certain extent. In the future, fluorescence laser confocal microscopy can be used to quantitatively analyze the fluorescence of CDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions, with a view to achieving the purpose of quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions in cells. Thus, it greatly broadens its application prospects in the field of biological fluorescence sensing.

In summary, we have proposed an easy, green and economic route for fabricating fluorescent CDs with the use of natural mustard seeds as the sole precursor. The as-prepared CDs show good fluorescence without further surface passivation treatment or the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials, with a high QY of up to 17.92 % and stability to strong acids, high ionic strength, and wide temperature range. Owing to their intriguing optical properties, these CDs were employed as a fluorescence probe for sensing of Fe3+ ions both in environmental water and Hok cells. More importantly, we have conducted a careful study on the quenching mechanism by means of UV–vis, FTIR, XPS, XRD, TEM, SEM, and fluorescent lifetime experiments.The results of XRD, XPS, FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescence lifetime confirmed that the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by Fe3+ ions was static quenching, and several evidences have been provided powerfully that the ground state complex formed between CDs and Fe3+ ions is non-fluorescent. The results of TEM and SEM provided direct evidence of aggregation-induced quenching of the fluorescence of CDs. In a word, it has been proven that fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ ions is a result of aggregation-induced and static quenching. In addition, we found that this CDs-based fluorescent probes have good application potential in environmental water and Hok cells. In the future, this green CDs with good fluorescence properties and stability may also shine in biomedical fields such as antibacterial and osteogenic applications.

Qingmei Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Songjie He: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Kaiwen Zheng: Resources, Methodology, Validation. Lupeng Zhang: Resources, Methodology, Validation. Lihua Lin: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fenghua Chen: Data curation, Supervision. Xiujuan Du: Data curation, Supervision. Bing Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the funding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11704274, 11705124), Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Quantum Manipulation and New Energy Materials(QMNEM1801), The Emerging Industry Leadership Talent Program of Shanxi Province (2019042), The Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province（No.201901D111267）and the Key R& D Project of Shanxi Province (201903D321111).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.110034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
